title: What the World’s First 100% Organic Wine Harvest Can Teach Us
summary:
For Penedès Denominació D’Origen, going organic required wrangling 160 cellars and some 2,000 individual grape growers.

The post What the World’s First 100% Organic Wine Harvest Can Teach Us appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
In 2020, the winemakers of Penedès Denominació D’Origen (DO Penedès) set out to achieve a rather lofty goal: go 100% organic by 2025.

Of course, this was ambitious, primarily because of scale. Penedès is not a small wine region. Going whole-hog organic would require wrangling 160 cellars and some 2,000 individual grape growers who oversee 4,509 hectares of vines and produce 15 million bottles.

But, in the fall of 2025, DO Penedès completed its first 100% organic harvest—the first DO in the world to do so. Every single bottle labeled under the DO was born from organic soils (with no synthetic chemical fertilizers, chemical pesticides, or herbicides) and vinified following organic practices.

“I never thought we would arrive at a moment like this,” says Josep M. Albet, of Albet i Noya, the first Penedès producer to practice organics. “I was convinced it was in the future, but I never intended it to run so fast.”

So, what exactly led to the appellation-wide decision? Consumer demand? Responsibility to the planet? Just a general do-gooder attitude?

More importantly, is this virtuous harvest repeatable, and will wine regions around the world follow suit?

Committing to the Bit

There are a number of catalysts that pushed the Catalonian DO towards organics.

For one, it’s prudent for the DO’s longevity. Climate change continues (and will continue) to upend how producers operate, which adds an overarching sense of uncertainty to the industry.

Going organic can feel like future-proofing—fortifying soils and vines so they can survive what may come down the road.

“It’s our small contribution to climate change,” says Joaquin Gay de Montellà Estany of Torre del Veguer, which has been operating since 1359. “It’s what we can do for our planet.”

“And if we don’t start doing things differently, we’re not going to be able to make wine in the future,” adds Sumarroca’s Marc Picon.

At the same time, going organic can also be lucrative. Most consumers are willing to pay more for products that are sustainable. This rings truer than ever for Gen Z consumers,a notoriously tough crowd for wine to entice. 

However, for passionate producers who come from a long line of Penedès viticulturalists, choosing to go organic is an extension of their roles as caretakers and stewards of what their parents passed down. 75% of DO Penedès wines are consumed within the region, so rallying behind locality matters.

“It’s very important for us to respect traditions,” says Maite Esteve of Vins El Cep, which has been farming biodynamically since 2003

Sticking Out in a Crowded Field

In Catalonia, identity is a confusing topic. Within the region’s geographical confines are twelve different DOs, including Priorat, Costers del Segre, Montsant, and Cava (which can be produced in over 20 regions, though Catalonia remains the spiritual home). With producers leaving DO Cava in favor of trade organization classifications like Corpinnat, things splinter even further. DO Penedès needed a clearer voice.

“We’ve always been in the shadow of DO Cava and DO Catalonia, who bring in bulk wines,” says Joan Huguet, who oversees the more than 600-year-old Bodega Huguet de Can Feixes and is president of DO Penedès.

While sparkling wine made Penedès popular, it also led to unsustainable levels of overproduction, resulting in cost-cutting measures being implemented to balance supply and demand. 

As the years passed, local producers felt uneasy about the state of the region. Higher-quality sparkling winemakers were able to defect to Corpinnat’s designation. But what were still wine producers to do?

DO Penedès took a hard look inward, examining its successes and failures, to try and figure out what its hallmark should be. The answer? Organics. 

At first, not everyone was on board. An exodus of producers—almost entirely co-operatives and large-scale wineries focused on volume—followed, unwilling to convert to organic production for financial reasons and profit security in an uncertain climate.

“We tried to talk with some but they’re making 50 million bottles—organics just isn’t a reality,” says Huguet. “They can’t afford to have volumes fall.”

But, to a region now focused on embracing history and quality, does volume even matter? Who cares, other than accounting? “We’re trying to add value, quality that matches the wine and the landscape,” says Huguet. “That is the future. If you are not organic, you will have a local wine with no personality, no origin.”

It’s Easy Being Green

The thing is, Penedès is well-suited for sustainability. “The weather is fantastic here,” says Huguet. “We’re 600 meters above sea level, and right by the sea.”

Disease pressure isn’t extreme, and threats of wildfire, frost, and other climate disasters are low.

However, drought and mildew have become real problems. In 2020, Finca Espiells lost 70% of their crop to mildew. “It’s difficult to treat it with copper,” says technical director José “Pepe” Hidalgo.

What do you use in place of copper? As producers try to get on the same organic page, Penedès has become a breeding ground for big conversations. How are you approaching pruning? What grapes are you planting? Is it working? 

“The climate is changing,” says Nuria Artiaga of Recaredo. “We need to question ourselves and keep learning. We need to take bets, right?”

From a varietal standpoint, too, Penedès has a home-field advantage, as producers can lean on indigenous grapes. Xarel-lo has become a poster child for Penedès, thanks not only to its compatibility with the Spanish climate but also to its adaptability—it can be bright and beachy, or left on the skins to make something more savory and textural. Many are also working with Sumoll, Malvasia di Sitges, Macabeu, and Xarel-lo Vermell (a red-skinned mutation of Xarel-lo).

Familia Torres, meanwhile, has been instrumental in reintroducing lost varieties like Forcada (a bright, saline-driven white) and Moneu (Gamay-ish, with a Spanish accent). Albet i Noya is intrigued with hybrid varieties, specifically ones bred nearby.

“We don’t have Pinot Noir. We don’t have Chardonnay,” says Artiaga. “Local varieties are what will grow best in our climate.”

Will similar transitions work for other wine regions? Maybe not. But any efforts to prioritize land health, especially while fortifying cultural identity, is worth praising.

Of course, there are growing pains, but they’re figuring it out, day by day. Ups and downs aside, DO Penedès is in it for the long run.

title: Wine Enthusiast’s Reviewers Taught Me to Taste Wine Like a Pro
summary:
Director of Digital Product Jessyca Frederick visited Wine Enthusiast HQ to learn how to calibrate wine like our writers at large.

The post Wine Enthusiast’s Reviewers Taught Me to Taste Wine Like a Pro appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Everyone I know (who doesn’t work at Wine Enthusiast) thinks I’m a wine expert. I’m not. I’m pretty knowledgeable, but mostly about the wines and regions I like best. I’m more like a prosumer.

I’ve tasted at more than one hundred wineries around the U.S. and Canada, and I’ve read enough wine reviews of my favorite producers and regions to know which critics are well-aligned with my own preferences. When I go wine tasting, I buy based on what I like. But when I spot a great deal at Costco, I check and defer to the scores.

My role at Wine Enthusiast includes a lot of number crunching. I’m a data nerd, and one day I was toodling around in our database of reviews looking for hidden gem wines of surprising value—those with very high scores and very low price points.

I stumbled on a 92-point rated Pinot Noir that retails for $8 at major grocery stores. I was confused, to say the least, so I went right out and bought one to try it for myself. 

On the nose, the red fruit was pronounced, which seemed promising. On the palate, it wasn’t bad, but the fruit was overwhelmed by vanilla—a common note for wines vinified with American Oak. I don’t usually like when my wine tastes like its winemaking technique—too much oak, too much malolactic fermentation, too much carbonic maceration, the list goes on. I prefer wines built in the vineyard, where the grape is the star, rather than ones manipulated at the winery.

When Monday rolled around, I messaged Anna-Christina Cabrales, WE’s Tasting Director. “How is it possible that this wine scored 92 points when a more premium Pinot Noir got the same score from another critic?”

“I’ve been getting this question a bit,” she said. “Which makes me wonder if we need to deep dive a little into how we score?”

Cabrales went on to say that people think reviewers give scores based on their personal preferences—which is not the case. “I think that our system is unique because [one reviewer] can love a wine, meanwhile I might find it gross and still rate it the same score or at least +/- a point from each other.”

It all comes down to calibration, she told me. I’m intrigued. “I personally want to know more about this calibration business,” I say.

We set a date.

The Calibration

On my next trip to the office, Cabrales generously welcomed my uninitiated tastebuds into the tasting room, alongside WE Writer-at-Large Tonya Pitts, a veteran sommelier and hospitality pro. 

The space, which smells surprisingly similar to a winery production facility, is surrounded by floor-to-ceiling natural wood racks with wines waiting to be reviewed. It’s a chilly 55-degrees to keep the bottles in pristine condition. While the pros had their “winemaker vests” on, as an inhabitant of the always sunny California desert, I was freezing. I suited up in my winter coat. 

Within moments, Senior Tasting Coordinator Russell Peborde lined up four mystery pours of white wine along with four bottles in numbered brown paper bags.

Cabrales gave us two minutes to move through each of the four wines. Our reviewers taste up to 100 wines in a sitting, so these tastings are often limited to 30-60 seconds per glass. As I sat next to these formidable women—racking my brain for something interesting to say—the silence was intimidating.

We wrote down some top-level notes and gave each wine a preliminary score. Then, we got down to brass tacks, diving into the details in each bottle. This is the calibration. 

I smelled butterscotch and ripe stone fruit, with a touch of minerality, while Pitts’ more sophisticated palate called out caramelized banana chips from “tons of oak,” she said.

“Let’s focus on the objective characteristics,” said Cabrales. “What sensations are you getting on the palate?”

Swirling, sniffing, and sipping anew, I taste “no oak, hot, and oily, with medium acidity and a medium finish.” What I refer to as bitter, Pitts calls “dirty.” She’s a more astute observer of flaws, so she can easily taste winery issues, like an unclean tank, and how that shows up in the final wine. 

The verdict? 84 points.

The second wine led me into the same trap, waxing poetic about aromas and flavors like petrol and underripe green apple. Again, I skipped the analysis of the wine’s structure, complexity, and mouthfeel. After some discussion, I resolved that the wine was astringent, had a short finish, a low level of complexity, and medium acidity.

This was an 86, according to Cabrales

On to number three.

While I couldn’t help but assess the aroma and taste first, I had learned better and wrote down information about the structure of the wine. Structure typically refers to the balance of acid, tannin, alcohol, and body that holds the wine together as it ages.

I found this wine to have medium to medium-plus acid with a little too much alcohol, but it had a complexity that appealed to me: aromas of white flowers, something toasty or bready, and a touch of citrus. I gave it an 87—I’d cheerfully quaff this baby on any sunny afternoon.

Cabrales and Pitts decided it was an 85. What did my amateur palate miss? We learned we’d been drinking Albariño from Spain. These wines were missing the typical complexity and aromatics, balanced mouthfeel, and bright acidity of the grape—hence the 84-86 point scores.

Wine Scoring at Wine Enthusiast

All tastings at Wine Enthusiast are conducted with our Aria short-stem universal wine glasses. Their minimalist build and razor-thin edge make it feel like “there’s nothing between you and the wine,” Cabrales says.

Our formal scoring rubric is as follows:

  • 98-100: CLASSIC The pinnacle of quality
  • 94-97: SUPERB A great achievement
  • 90-93: EXCELLENT Highly recommended
  • 87-89: VERY GOOD Often good value; well recommended
  • 83-86: GOOD Suitable for everyday consumption; often good value
  • 80-82: ACCEPTABLE Can be employed in casual, less-critical circumstances
  • Reviews for products that receive a rating below 80 points are not published

After a detailed explanation of those markers, Anna-Christina explained that the baseline score for each wine is 87. Examples of wines that receive an 87 would be those without major flaws, but without much character, either. The more flaws you find, the lower the score; the more character and signs of quality or ageability you find, the higher the score.

The Rest of the Experience

As we moved on to the second tasting group, a series of three sparkling wines, we tried to focus on what was essential—acidity, finish, alcohol, and balance. Our scores were all within a point of each other, which suggested we’d learned something from the previous batch.

The calibration had concluded, but several other Wine Enthusiast team members filtered in to join the wine-nerd fun. Marshall Tilden III, our president of wine education and resident WSET Academy instructor, brought along a selection of Chardonnays he was negotiating to sell in our online wine store, Wine Express. There were a variety of price-points, regions, and styles on the table. But, while I kept going back to the Matanzas Creek Chardonnay which sang on my own palate, I was impressed with how aligned Tilden and Cabrales were with their scores—calibration in action.

This was a big day for me. It challenged ideas about wine criticism I’d clung to for a long time. 

Previously, I assumed that if one publication or critic gave a wine a 94, but others gave it a 92, that the reviewer who gave it a 94 is probably biased in some way. After learning about calibration, I wonder what that reviewer saw in the wine that the others missed. Did they notice more complexity or ageability the others overlooked? Or, alternatively, what did the bestower of the 94 overlook that others called out? 

Before, varying scores confused me. Now, they make me want to buy those bottles to judge for myself.

Do I have goals of becoming a wine critic? No—but I do want to retain my street cred as a savvy wine shopper.

title: Beyond Red Drink: Wine Pros Share What Bottles They’re Pouring This Juneteenth
summary:
Sommeliers and beverage professionals share the bottles they’re popping to celebrate Juneteenth this year.

The post Beyond Red Drink: Wine Pros Share What Bottles They’re Pouring This Juneteenth appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
When Juneteenth was officially declared a federal holiday in 2021, it codified what Black Americans knew all along. Commemorating the historic moment on June 19, 1865, when enslaved Black Americans in Galveston, Texas, were notified of their emancipation more than two and a half years after the Emancipation Proclamation, the day is one of the most important in American history.

Since then, Black communities have held annual Juneteenth celebrations as a way to honor the struggles of the past, while celebrating a long overdue freedom. As Black Americans migrated to every corner of the country, these gatherings evolved into a breadth of events. In the South and other more rural areas, celebrations were centered around the church and community festivals with fried fish and folding chairs, whereas Juneteenth festivities in Harlem and Brooklyn were reimagined as block parties up and down brownstone-lined streets.

But no matter the setting, Juneteenth celebrations were incomplete without a sizzling grill and some red juice. Harkening back to the traditional West African drink of garnet-colored hibiscus and kola teas, red juice symbolizes the bloodshed endured by enslaved Black Americans. The selection of red drinks proliferated, with Kool-Aid, fruit punch, and strawberry sodas all viable options.

“When I think of a Juneteenth jubilee, I’m taken back to early memories of my mom and aunts gathered together, glasses of Lambrusco or White Zinfandel in hand,” says sommelier and Texas native Monica Jones. “Wine, for me, has always been about community.” 

While today’s Juneteenth celebrations still center around red juice, the drinks lining the table are becoming more varied, from chilled reds to food-friendly rosés. These wine professionals share what they’re pouring—and why—this Juneteenth.

Chilled Reds

Chilled red wine is the antidote for sunny summer days that transition into balmy evenings. Sommelier Mahlik Richardson will reach for a chilled red on Juneteenth as a thoughtful way to honor the tradition of red drinks. “Juneteenth is a relatively new national celebration, which means we’re actively shaping what those traditions look like,” he says. 

He plans on opening a bottle of Where’s Linus? Red, a collaboration between winemaker Chris Christenson and importer Jenny & François that hovers somewhere between juicy and jammy. 

“Not only is it produced by a Black winemaker, but it’s a great pairing option for everything from grilled meats to lighter sides,” says Richardson.

Chilled reds are also Jones’ go-to for Juneteenth. She recommends lightly chilling a bottle for 15 minutes before enjoying it. (She’ll be reaching for House of Brown’s red blend.)

For more bottle inspiration, check out our favorite chilled reds for barbecues.

Rosé

Lighter in body, but still structured enough to withstand hearty soul food dishes typically eaten on Juneteenth, sommelier Loris Jones-Randolph recommends rosé. It’s a thoughtful nod to red-tinged tradition while offering something more refreshing.

Jones-Randolph has been enjoying Generational Wealth Wines’ The Rosecrans Rosé. Named after a street in South Central Los Angeles, the rosé is owner Keyonna Kidd-Goins’ love letter to the energy and spirit of her city. “There’s something special about how the wine itself is a bridge of cultures, made by a Black woman who wanted to see herself and her neighborhood represented in the industry,” says Jones-Randolph. 

Peruse our top-rated rosés from around the world.

White Bordeaux

Structured whites, although a deviation from red drinks typical of Juneteenth, have elbowed their way onto the table as a perfect pairing for grilled food. For Tuanni Price, wine educator and founder of Zuri Wine Tasting and Afro Wine Week, a classic white Bordeaux blend conjures up feelings of both history and optimism for Juneteenth. 

“Château Fillon White Bordeaux is a classic blend of Sauvignon Blanc and Sémillon, but what makes it truly special is the heritage behind it,” says Price. “The Château is owned by a Black Haitian-American family, so opening a bottle feels like sipping history. I’m personally reaching for it because it embodies the spirit of Juneteenth—celebrating excellence, legacy, and the deep roots of the diaspora.” 

Here’s a beginner’s guide to the stunning diversity of White Bordeaux.

Sparkling

The pop of a cork is an especially festive way to kick off Juneteenth shenanigans, making sparkling wine an increasingly popular choice for the occasion. Jones-Randolph opts for sparklers as a natural crowd-pleaser that’s perfect for mid-June weather. Plus, bubbles abound with food pairing possibilities for a large group. 

“Brenae Royale, vintner of Veraison Project, makes a NV sparkling wine from Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Pinot Meunier, and Sauvignon Blanc grapes in charmat method, with the proceeds going towards making sure there are more Black women and men in wine,” says Jones-Randolph. 

Take a look at the best sparkling wines from around the world.

Skin Contact

With its proclivity for bridging the acidity of white wine with the food-friendly tannins of a red, skin-contact is an easy choice for Juneteenth celebrations. “If I’m given the opportunity to pick a fun wine, no matter the occasion, skin-contact is always a great choice,” says Richardson.

This Juneteenth, he recommends Aslina’s 2023 Skin Contact Chenin, for not only its food friendliness, but also the narrative thread it draws from hardship to success. Ntsiki Biyela trudged through discrimination in post-apartheid South Africa to emerge as the country’s first Black female winemaker. After obtaining a degree in Agriculture (Viticulture and Oenology), Biyela started her label, Aslina, in 2016.

title: Support Your Favorite World Cup Team with These Knockout Cocktails
summary:
Kick off the international soccer tournament with these 26 drinks from competing countries.

The post Support Your Favorite World Cup Team with These Knockout Cocktails appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Soccer fans, sports enthusiasts, and good-time-lovers rejoice! On June 11th, the FIFA World Cup returns after four long years. Now in its 23rd run, the sweeping soccer tournament is coming to North America, where cities in Canada, Mexico, and the U.S. will host highly anticipated matches—and massive numbers of tourists.

What better way to get into the spirit than some good old-fashioned adult beverages? We’ve taken the liberty of pairing drinks with 25 of the countries participating in the tournament. 

From Brazilian caipirinhas and Spanish kalimotxo to American Manhattans and martinis, whip up one of your team’s top national beverages to show your support. You can switch it up game-to-game, choosing the team you hope wins each match (or the cocktail best suited to your tastes). Just like every team gets a shot at a big win, will you check off every cocktail on the list?

However you decide to play it, these drinks will ensure that your World Cup celebrations are triumphant—regardless of what happens on the field.

Lemon Ace

Australia

Yes, the Lemon Ace was technically created for tennis’ Australian Open, but hear us out: The sparkling lemonade and tart passionfruit syrup make this drink a supremely refreshing cocktail ready to liven up any summertime spectator sport.

White Russian

Belgium

Despite this drink’s name (and Big Lebowski fame), the White Russian’s origins are actually found in Belgium, man. If you like a sweet and strong bev, this is the one for you—just swirl together equal parts vodka and coffee liqueur, and top with a splash of cream.

Caipirinha

Brazil

Cachaça, an alcohol distilled from sugar cane, is the shining star of the Caipirinha, Brazil’s national cocktail. The other ingredients—lime juice and simple syrup—are kept intentionally simple in order to let the spirit shine. It’s fresh, lowkey, and just might be one of the best things you can drink to beat the heat (as long as you hydrate, too).

Maple Bacon Old Fashioned

Canada

There’s nothing more stereotypically Canadian than maple syrup, eh? This Maple Bacon Old Fashioned uses maple syrup in place of the classic sugar cube and tops the glass with a crisp slice of bacon. Sub in Canadian bacon if you’re feeling extra crazy.

Blue Lagoon

Curaçao

Don’t let the Blue Lagoon’s striking color scare you away. This cocktail mixes Curaçao’s namesake, orange-flavored liqueur with vodka and lemonade for a deceptively straightforward and thirst-quenching drink.

Cypriot Brandy Sour

Egypt

Originally made for Egypt’s King Farouk in the 1930s, the Cypriot Brandy Sour is a combination of Brandy, bitters, and “lemon squash,” essentially a lemon simple syrup with the perfect balance of tart and sweet. 

Pimm’s Cup

England

While it’s traditionally served at Wimbledon, we think the quintessentially British Pimm’s Cup would be right at home in a soccer stadium. It’s made with a 1:2 ratio of Pimm’s No. 1 liqueur and sparkling English lemonade, but feel free to use lemon-lime soda in a pinch—just don’t skimp on the garnishes. And if fruity drinks aren’t your vibe, we won’t fault you for going with a classic G&T.

French 75

France

If the name didn’t give it away, the origin of the classic French 75 can be traced back to France—Harry’s Bar in Paris to be exact. With gin, Champagne, lemon juice, and simple syrup, this is a sophisticated and sparkly delight.

Glühwein… Or Beer

Germany

Glühwein is a traditional German drink made by simmering red wine with a combination of orange juice, sugar, and warming spices. It hits hard on a cool day, but if you’re (understandably) not in the mood to drink hot wine this summer, keep things simple with our favorite German beers.

Kremas

Haiti

Kremas is a celebratory beverage whose recipe has been passed down through Haitian families for centuries. Combining rum, coconut milk, cream of coconut, evaporated milk, sweetened condensed milk, and a slew of cozy spices, this is creamy perfection over ice.

Doogh

Iran

A classic Iranian drink, Doogh is the perfect choice for anyone looking for a non-alcoholic beverage option to enjoy this summer. Just mix greek yogurt with mint, salt, and sparkling water for a lip-smacking refreshment with a touch of salinity.

Mizuwari

Japan

In the mood to get technical? This is the cocktail for you. The Mizuwari requires swirling Japanese whiskey clockwise exactly 13 ½ times before adding sparkling water, then swirling (clockwise) another 3 ½ times to blend.

Margarita

Mexico

Although details on its original creator are hotly debated, it’s highly likely that the classic margarita dates back to 1930s Mexico. Almost a century later, it’s one of the most popular cocktails across the globe—for good reason. The classic shakes together blanco tequila, orange liqueur, and lime for an utterly satisfying sipper.

Tangerine-Mint Prosecco Freeze

Morocco

Mint and tangerine are classic Moroccan flavors, and they find an easy home in this tangerine-mint Prosecco freeze. Cooling, minty, and lightly sweet, this recipe makes pulling out the ice cream machine more than worth it.

Sum Nice, Sum Spice

The Netherlands

Genever is a gin-like spirit that’s exclusively made in the Netherlands, Belgium, and certain parts of France and Germany. Described as “spicy spa water,” the Sum Nice, Sum Spice cocktail contrasts genever’s sweet and malty profile with cooling mint and cucumber and an added kick of spicy serrano.

Gefion

Norway

Norway’s Linie aquavit is wholly unique—it ages in old sherry casks while sailing across the world and crossing the equator twice. Named for a Norse goddess, the Gefion cocktail combines Linie with rye whiskey and Amaro Montenegro for an Old-Fashioned-esque drink that’s equally herbal and aromatic.

Sauvignon Blanc

New Zealand

New Zealand fans, skip the cocktails and go straight for some good ‘ole New Zealand Sauvy B. Feeling festive? Throw in some jalapeño slices for a little extra oomph.

The Port World Cup

Portugal

The Port World Cup literally has ‘World Cup’ in its name, so what kind of magazine would we be if we left it off this list? Naturally, we’ve paired this bev with Portugal for its hefty serving of Tawny port, but the inclusion of rum, a Czech herbal liquor, lime juice, and simple syrup make this a truly global cocktail.

Rob Roy

Scotland

Why mess with perfection? A nice pour of Scotch whisky is celebratory on its own. But if you want to mix things up, we suggest the pre-Prohibition Rob Roy with a hint of Drambuie and an optional dash of Angostura bitters.

Kalimotxo

Spain

The Kalimotxo, also known as the Calimocho, is beloved across Spain. Made with a 3:1 ratio of red wine and cola, this cocktail is as simple as it is refreshing.

Northern Sazerac

Sweden

In this Scandinavian twist on a classic Sazerac, Spirit of Hven Tycho’s Star, a single malt whiskey from Sweden, is subbed in place of rye. The Northern Sazerac is a smooth sipper with the ideal balance of smokiness, maltiness, and complexity.

Death in the Afternoon

Switzerland

Although the historic Death in the Afternoon cocktail created by Ernest Hemingway is believed to have roots in Paris, the two-ingredient drink relies heavily on absinthe, so we’re going with Switzerland here. When swirled with Champagne, the Swiss spirit takes on a light effervescence that perfectly complements its licorice flavor profile.

Manhattan… or Martini

United States

The true origins of the Manhattan are hazy, but it’s indisputably tied to the borough for which it was named. With a potent combo of rye whiskey, sweet vermouth, and bitters, it’s difficult to find anything better. But, if you’re looking for something with a little more lift, the classic gin martini is equally delicious and well-loved across the country.

Clericó

Uruguay

Clericó is Uruguay’s take on sangria, and it’s been a summertime staple in the country for centuries. Traditionally, juicy fruits like strawberries, mangoes, and peaches are mashed, then soaked with dry white wine, orange liqueur, and a dash of lemon-lime soda.

World Cup Cocktail

If all else fails…

Listen, we’re not exactly sure whether the World Cup Cocktail’s origins have anything to do with the tournament, but it needed to be on this list. This way, you can still get in on the fun if your country is eliminated from the bracket. Combining cachaça, amaro, and citrus, this cocktail is zesty deliciousness, win or lose.

title: ‘Top Chef’ Shirley Chung’s Most Prized Bottle Is a Symbol of Survival and Support
summary:
The boutique Napa Valley Cab was made by a close friend to help raise funds for the chef's battle with tongue cancer.

The post ‘Top Chef’ Shirley Chung’s Most Prized Bottle Is a Symbol of Survival and Support appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Chinese-American Chef Shirley Chung was born in Beijing, where she developed her love of food at an early age. After moving to California, she attended culinary school, training in classic French and Italian cuisine and ultimately landing work in the kitchens of esteemed chef Thomas Keller. Chung became known as the “Dumpling Queen” of Los Angeles thanks to appearances on Bravo’s Top Chef (seasons 11 and 14), and has become a tireless advocate for the AAPI community. 

Recently, she overcame late-stage tongue cancer, adding another distinctive title to her CV: warrior. Almost two years ago, in the summer of 2024, Chung was forced to close her Los Angeles restaurant, Ms. Chi Cafe, to focus on her health after the life-changing diagnosis. On the advice of chef Grant Achatz, who had gone through a similar course of treatment in the Windy City, she moved to Chicago with her husband and prepared for battle. 

“Chef Grant has been my chef idol since I started cooking,” she says. “He judged me in Top Chef Season 11 in the season finale. I remember: he made me so nervous but he loved my food. When I was diagnosed, I reached out and he answered right away. He told me to take one day at a time and take one task at a time. And that’s exactly what I did… It was great advice. And also, [he told me to]fight like a chef!”

Chung needed a feeding tube throughout her treatment and had to relearn how to speak, swallow, and use her palate. A year and a half after ending treatment—without recurrence—she is infectiously optimistic.

Earlier this year, it was time for Chung to stage her professional comeback. She opened Night Rooster in Dallas’ Design District in January, where her signature dumplings are the star of the menu. 

She is still retraining her palate, regaining her strength, and seeing a speech and swallow therapist. But her energy is positive and grateful. Her chef community was an incredible support system throughout her treatment, and she’s already paying it forward, acting as a resource for people with similar diagnoses. 

Through it all, Chung’s enthusiasm about food and wine never waned, and, if anything, feels that her palate could be sharper now than ever before. 

“After radiation treatment, I feel like my palate can really dissect really intricate flavors—it’s surprising,” she says. “I pick up on little details in flavors I didn’t taste before, the subtle flavors, the complexities, the small details. I don’t know if it’s my brain spending more energy on my palate, or maybe my palate is more sensitive to every single thing that I’m tasting. Maybe it’s a blessing in disguise!”

Owner: Shirley Chung, chef (Night Rooster in Dallas, TX), Dumpling Queen, cookbook author, chef contestant (Top Chef, Top Chef Duels, Tournament of Champions), warrior

Bottle: 2017 Napa Valley WS Dumpling Mafia Cabernet Sauvignon

How’d I Get It? “I’ve always enjoyed wine even though when it comes to alcohol, I’m a lightweight. Being East Asian, I’m a little bit allergic, but wine is a little easier for me than hard liquor, so I prefer it. I love bubbles to start with a meal, including Chinese food. I love a very balanced California Cab. Because I spent my entire career on the West Coast and cooked in wine country, I’ve always preferred California wines. 

My bottle is a special edition of Cabernet from a boutique winemaker from Napa called Wine Stash. They don’t sell to many people; it’s my friend Michael Stubelt’s passion project. A group of chef friends and Guy Fieri threw me a fundraiser. My husband and I had no income for many months and we’re paying for homes in two cities and for my treatment out of pocket. And so my dear friend, the winemaker from Wine Stash, wanted to be a part of the fundraiser and wanted to make a special wine with me for this event—my private label. He made three magnum bottles of our blend and everyone signed it (all the chefs and donors, everybody at the event) and he auctioned off the giant bottles and gave a smaller bottle to me. 

Michael knows me very well and knows how I feel about food and wine and my preferences. When it comes to Cab, I like a cross between a Big Napa Cab with Old World depth and complexity; I love the smokiness, I love the earthiness. 

So this special blend—not only did he source the grapes from the prestigious George III Vineyard in the heart of Rutherford Napa Valley, the wine was in three different oak barrels in order to have that complexity I love—to match the way I cook. That pursuit of greatness [in winemaking] very much matches my passion when it comes to food. That craftsmanship, that heart, chasing your passion, really matches with who I am. 

After my recovery, the first sip of alcohol I tasted was this vintage, this bottle. (I didn’t open my bottle, they sent me a sample).”

Purchase Price: Gifted 

Current Estimated Value: $100

When Do You Plan to Drink It? “The bottle is on display in my home on top of my wine cabinet. Every single chef at my fundraiser, everyone who attended, any major donor all signed the bottle. So it’s a bottle filled with love and support, and it’s my driving force: all that love coming into a bottle to push me forward. 

It pushed me forward to take on all the treatment and recover—and open restaurants, compete, go back and live my life even fuller. Without it I wouldn’t be able to live as Shirley 2.0 today. I hope to drink this wine on my 100th birthday (my family is known for longevity) with the winemakers, my loved ones, my manager Lisa Shotland, who has been my backbone throughout my cancer journey, and also, Guy Fieri (laughs). The amount of people who came out to support me, it was unbelievable.”

title: Make the Glamorous Whisky Cocktail Served at the Tony Awards
summary:
If your invite for the 79th Annual Tony Awards got lost in the mail, we have you covered with this 'Curtain Call' recipe from Broadway’s most exclusive party.

The post Make the Glamorous Whisky Cocktail Served at the Tony Awards appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Not many people know that Broadway’s biggest night is also an occasion to try the best food and drink that New York City has to offer. At the official afterparty following the 79th Annual Tony Awards, nominees, presenters, and VIP guests can mix and mingle over cheese-dusted fried chicken bites from Naro, cinnamon crullers from Daily Provisions, wagyu Chicago-style hot dogs from 5 Acres, and an array of one-night-only cocktails from the award show’s official sponsor, Dewar’s Scotch Whisky.

Of course, there will also be cocktails at the actual Tony Awards, which takes place at Radio City Music Hall. Tucked away within the venue’s iconic Art Deco balcony lookouts is a swanky speakeasy, which guests can visit while waiting for the show to start. Cocktails available at the Baccarat x Dewar’s Scotch Whisky Speakeasy include the Curtain Call, an elegant sour-style drink with blood orange purée, and the 21st Measure, an Old Fashioned riff with chocolate mole bitters and Bénédictine. 

If your invite for the 79th Annual Tony Awards got lost in the mail, we have you covered with this cocktail recipe from Broadway’s most exclusive party.

The Curtain Call

From Damiano Coren of NYC’s Casa Lever

Ingredients

2 ounces Dewar’s 21-Year-Old Oloroso
0.25 ounce Kirsch
0.25 ounce Lemon Juice
0.25 ounce Simple Syrup
1 ounce Blood Orange Puree
0.5 ounce Noyaux 5 Dashes Fee Foam
Garnish: Lemon peel, raspberry, blackberry

Directions

Shake and double strain.

title: How to Measure Your Space for a Built-In Wine Cooler
summary:
Nothing ruins the excitement of a new wine cooler faster than discovering the door can only open halfway because it’s smacking into the kitchen island.

The post How to Measure Your Space for a Built-In Wine Cooler appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
There comes a point in every wine lover’s life when the “couple bottles on the counter” situation starts getting a little out of hand. 

Maybe your kitchen suddenly has more Pinot Noir than pantry space. Maybe you’re tired of white wines fighting for room next to leftover takeout in the refrigerator. Either way, upgrading to a built-in wine cooler feels like a pretty serious life achievement.

But where is the wine cooler going to go, and are you sure it’s going to be the right fit? Luckily you don’t need to be a contractor or someone who casually uses phrases like “load-bearing wall” to ensure you handle this part properly. You just need a tape measure, a few minutes, a little patience, and the willingness to crawl under a counter and get a little dirty.

And trust me, it’s worth doing right the first time. Nothing ruins the excitement of a new wine cooler faster than discovering the door can only open halfway because it’s smacking into the kitchen island. That’s why, around here, we follow the “measure twice, cut once” mantra. Here are some simple steps that will ensure that you not only select the right wine cooler, but make sure it fits like a glove in your intended space.

Built-In vs. Freestanding

Before you start measuring, make sure you’re shopping for the correct type of wine cooler.

A built-in wine cooler is designed to fit seamlessly under counters or inside cabinetry. These vent from the front, which allows heat to escape properly even in tighter spaces. Most are zero clearance, but some will need minimal ventilation for fresh air intake, so be sure to check on that before you measure.

A freestanding wine cooler needs open air (typically 2-3 inches) around the sides and back to breathe. Trying to wedge one into cabinetry is basically asking it to overheat before your rosé even gets chilled, and can honestly be a little dangerous.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Prestige L Dual Zone Smart Wi-Fi Wine Cellar

In Stock | $2,499

Shop Now

If you’re going for that clean, integrated kitchen look, then going with a built-in wine cooler is 100% the move.

What You’ll Need

This is one of those rare home projects where you probably already own everything required, and if not you can order any of these on Amazon and get them in a jiffy.

  • Maybe a second person, if kneeling on kitchen tile isn’t your favorite hobby
  • A tape measure (or a well-rated measure app)
  • A flashlight (yep, even the one on your phone)
  • A notepad or Notes app
  • Painter’s tape if you want to map things out visually

Step 1: Clear the Space

Clear and clean the area so it is as empty and open as possible. This will allow you to measure the true inside opening and your usable cabinet space.

Step 2: Measure 

Start by measuring the width of the opening from left to right.

Here’s the important part—measure in three places:

  • Front
  • Middle
  • Back

Cabinets and floors are typically not always perfectly straight, especially in older homes or city apartments where things tend to shift over time.

Use the smallest measurement you get. So, if the front says 15 inches but the back says 14.75, congratulations—you officially have a 14.75-inch opening. Cabinets are unforgiving like that.

Next, measure the height from the floor to the underside of the countertop. Again, check a few different spots. Floors can slope slightly, and sometimes previous renovations create tiny differences you won’t notice until installation day.If new flooring was installed after the cabinets went in, you may have lost a little clearance height without realizing it. And don’t try to force an exact fit. Give yourself a little breathing room—a wine cooler should slide into that space as smooth as well-aged Bordeaux.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Latitude S 24″ Smart Wi-Fi Undercounter Wine Cellar

In Stock | $1,149

Shop Now

Last is depth, which can be the trickiest. Measure from the back wall to the front edge of the cabinet or countertop, then compare it carefully to the cooler’s listed depth. And again, measure at the floor, in the middle, and towards the top. You also want a measurement that takes into account the handles. A sleek stainless handle can stick out farther than expected, especially in narrower kitchens where every inch matters.

Also check to see what’s hiding on that back wall where the back of the unit will slide into. Items like electrical outlets, baseboard molding, and plumbing lines can affect how far back the unit can actually go.

A pro tip from Jerry Garcia, an experienced wine cellar designer with Wine Enthusiast, is to make sure you properly document these dimensions. 

“It’s a good idea to snap a picture of the space and write the measurements on it using an editing app,” he says. “This makes it easy to share with your design consultant or others that may be helping with the project and installation.”

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast VinoView 24” Smart Wi-Fi Undercounter Wine Cellar-Panel Ready

In Stock | $1,799

Shop Now

Step 3: Think About Door Clearance

A wine cooler door needs enough room to swing fully open so shelves can slide out properly. Otherwise, grabbing a bottle can become a frustrating game of Jenga. Take stock of narrow walkways, and things like kitchen islands, cabinets, and other doored-appliances that could cramp the space.

If you live in a tighter apartment or galley kitchen, painter’s tape is your best friend here. Tape out the dimensions of the open door on the floor and see how the space feels, because a door technically fitting and it comfortably fitting are two very different things. This is not something you want to learn the hard way.

In the Shop

N’FINITY PRO HDX 30″ Wine and Beverage Center

In Stock | $1,299

Shop Now

Step 4: Don’t Ignore Ventilation

Built-in wine coolers vent out from the front, and while many will take in fresh air from the same fan grill at the front of the unit, some may still need a little extra airflow to operate efficiently. Always check the manufacturer’s clearance recommendations, as certain units may require a bit of space behind or above the cooler. Even the slightest bit of breathing room not only helps performance and efficiency, but it boosts the longevity of the unit too. 

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Prestige L SingleZone Smart Wi-Fi Wine Cellar Stainless Steel Right Hinge

In Stock | $2,199

Shop Now

PSA: Common Pre-Installation Mistakes

Having helped thousands of wine collectors trying to fit a round peg into a square hole over the last two decades, these are the most common measuring mistakes that we see happen:

  • Measuring only once
  • Forgetting baseboards or trim
  • Ignoring door swing space
  • Assuming walls are perfectly square
  • Forgetting about handles and hinges
  • Not checking outlet placement
  • Buying based on “it should probably fit”

These are all easily avoidable, especially that last one, if you follow the simple guide provided here. Pro tip: Don’t assume that something will just work itself out; a unit won’t magically fit into a space that is too tight.

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Latitude Double S 24″ Smart Wi-Fi Undercounter Dual Zone Wine Cellar – Stainless Steel Door

In Stock | $2,269

Shop Now

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, measuring for a built-in wine cooler really comes down to slowing down and paying attention to the details. Once you break it into steps (like the ones above) it’s a pretty manageable project, even for those who avoid tape measures like Sideways’ Miles avoids Merlot.

I assure you—spending an extra ten minutes measuring in the beginning is a whole lot better than scratching up your floors (and your brand-new cooler) trying to make something fit.

More Wine Coverage

  • Read Marshall’s recent story on how to properly store your wine in the summer.
  • Winemakers are using unique bottle shapes to stand out the rosé market.
  • Here are the best wine pairings for summer BBQs, campfires, and more.
  • Regan Stephens’ latest story offers a peek into life as a postpartum drinks professional.
  • The next ‘IYKYK’ glass? Macvin du Jura.

title: There’s Far More to Australian Wine Than Critter Labels
summary:
The country's wine reputation may have taken a hit due to the proliferation of cheap, mass-produced bottles of the early 2000s, but the wines Down Under punch far above their weight.

The post There’s Far More to Australian Wine Than Critter Labels appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Few large scale wine-producing nations are as overlooked and underrated by Americans as Australia. The Yellowtail critter label craze of the early 2000s is one of many reasons why. The boom-and-bust scenario it created took its toll on the nation’s reputation in the States. But, despite the myriad challenges currently facing the industry (more on that later), Aussie wine has never been better—or more worthy of attention. 

There is, of course, rich, supple Barossa Valley Shiraz to sip beside a slab of steak. And there’s downright sexy Chardonnay—whether from Margaret River, Yarra Valley, or Tasmania—that strikes a sweet spot between seriously age-worthy and crowd-pleasing. But these only scratch the surface. 

With 65 designated wine regions scattered around the edges of a nation only slightly smaller than the United States, Australia’s wine scene is diverse, vibrant, and multifaceted. Over 160 wine varieties are cultivated by well-trained viticulturists and made by highly-experienced, globe-trotting winemakers in landscapes that range from hot, dry, and dusty to cool, wet, and mountainous, and everything in between. 

The vines, some of which are the planet’s oldest, grow in soils that pre-date the dinosaurs. They’re generally thoughtfully farmed and picked by workers who earn living wages. Australia is home to both the oldest continually produced wine in the world and also to many of its most forward-thinking “young gun” producers. The nation’s wine research institute is a world leader in climate change research, and its rigorous, expansive wine show system sets a very high quality bar. 

No matter their humble, “she’ll be ’right, mate” beach bum vibes. Aussies take wine very seriously. It’s reflected in the bottle.

Weather Report

Of the many challenges facing Australian wine at the moment, climate change is arguably most concerning. This past harvest, in the Hunter Valley in New South Wales, after the wettest winter in 60 years, summer’s hot, dry conditions have made for a fast and furious pick. Meanwhile, it reached a record-breaking 121°F in the inland South Australian Riverland wine region in February. In Victoria, the new year brought raging wildfires across normally cool inland regions like the Strathbogie Ranges, devastating vineyards, wineries, livestock, storage units, and family homes (Fowles Wines and Maison Lapalus, whose wines are both sold Stateside, could especially use support at the moment). 

It hasn’t been all-hot-all-the-time, though. The La Niña-influenced seasons of 2021-2023 brought cooler, wetter weather to South Australia and Victoria. While flooding became an issue in riverfront areas, many of the wines produced from these vintages—notably from the normally hot regions of South Australia—offer exceptional elegance and aromatics. 

Remember, Australia is sprawling. It may be scorching in one region and pouring rain in another. The island state of Tasmania experienced a significantly cool, late vintage in 2026, for example. 

The quality-led Margaret River region in the west has been fortunate enough to enjoy a lengthy run of good-to-exceptional vintages. Moderated by the nearby Indian Ocean, the region bears little resemblance to the weather patterns in Australia’s south and southeast.

While Australia will continue to face climactic challenges, it’s also well-placed to mitigate and adapt. Aussies are making—and will continue to make—many exceptional wines, even in the toughest vintages.

Trend Report

On paper, things don’t look so hot for the Australian wine industry. Sales are down domestically. And exports (which account for 59% of its wine sales, with more than 50% of that going to the U.S. and U.K.) have been in consistent decline for the last several years. The value of Aussie wine in the United States is at an all-time low. While exports to China, another major market, bounced back somewhat after the crippling tariffs placed on Australian wine by the nation in 2021, China’s overall wine consumption is down, just like everywhere else. And relations between the two nations are still precarious. 

A bumper crop in 2025 has compounded a worsening issue: Australia has more wine than it can sell (although it’s far from alone with this problem). In bulk-producing regions like the aforementioned Riverland, grapes are left unpicked on the vines. Growers are either offered pitiful sums for their fruit, or contracts are cancelled altogether. It’s not helped by the fact that two large multinational companies with huge stakes in Aussie wine who would normally snaffle up much of that surplus wine—namely Treasury Wine Estates (who own brands like Penfolds and Wynn’s) and Vinarchy, formerly known as Accolade (Hardy’s and Jacob’s Creek)—are in difficult financial situations themselves. The share price for Treasury dropped by 56% in 2025. Thankfully the 2026 vintage is looking far smaller, and the hope is that supply-demand levels will re-balance somewhat. 

Look beyond the numbers, though, and you’ll find an industry as vibrant and progressive as it’s ever been. After all, Australia led the world first in squeaky clean, stable winemaking via techniques like cold fermentation and reductive winemaking throughout the second half of the 20th century. Then, as the pendulum swung, Aussies were ahead of the curve in crafting funky, colorful natural wine in the aughts. Now it’s landed in a sweet in-the-middle spot. Large scale, commercial operators have adopted some of natty wine’s “hands-off” techniques like native yeast and oxidative ferments, resulting in more characterful wines. And natty producers have relented to some “conventional techniques,” like sulphur additions, for fault-free wines that still smack of artisanal creativity.  

Australia is the nation that kickstarted a screw cap revolution, invented the bag-in-box, and pioneered countless viticulture and oenological innovations. If anyone can pivot in hard times, it’s the Aussies.

And that’s exactly what they’re doing. From re-planting hotter regions like Riverland to more heat-and-drought tolerant varieties from Portugal and Italy and zeroing in on laser-focused whites in response to shifting palates, to re-imagining Shiraz through a modern, regenerative lens and celebrating the inherent gorgeousness of their old-vine Grenache, there’s a lot to be excited about Down Under. 

Futurecast

Australians’ resilience and ingenuity will be put to the test over the next several years as it faces myriad climactic and economic challenges. 

Producers will continue to seek out the coolest corners of the country. Regions like Tasmania, Great Southern, Adelaide Hills, and Gippsland—where fresh whites, sparkling, and light-on-their-feet reds like Pinot and Gamay are on the rise—will become even more central to the country’s success. Modern Grenache, in all its heady, fruit-and-floral, mid-weight glory, will play a starring role in South Australia’s wine scene. Finally, while some of it will get ripped up, producers will find creative ways to reinvent their most planted (and currently woefully unpopular) variety, Shiraz.

An alignment of brand and story with physical place, particularly through the tourism lens, will become increasingly crucial. Australia’s wineries are already leaders in the “cellar door” (Aussie for tasting room) experience, with many wineries offering top dining experiences, art exhibits, and other immersive adventures. In September 2025, in recognition of the nearly $36 billion (USD) the wine industry contributes to the nation’s economy, the Australian government extended its Wine Tourism and Cellar Door Grants program with another AUD $10 million (the equivalent of  nearly $7 million USD) in funding towards improving the tasting room experience. So expect even more push to get bums into tasting room seats. 

Innovative viticulture technology will continue to improve farming, with highly precise irrigation and soil health programs, better bushfire prevention, and smoke taint detection technology.

There will be casualties of these tough economic times—wineries and vineyards will shutter—but my hope is that in re-balancing supply and demand in a way that aligns with Australia’s changing environment, the nation’s wine industry will emerge more sustainable and resilient, with a stronger global spotlight on its best wines and producers.

What to Drink Right Now

Here are some recently reviewed Australian wines to suit everyone’s taste, from your steak-loving uncle to the too-cool-for-school twenty-something in your life.

South Australia

An Australian wine exploration must start with South Australia. Home to 18 of the nation’s 65 Geographically Indicated regions (GIs), “S.A.” is Aussie wine’s heartland. All across the south and southeast of the state, surrounding its main city, Adelaide, lies a vinous kingdom where gnarled 125 year old “ancestor” vines of Shiraz and Grenache reign on thrones of 200 million year old soils (some of the oldest on Planet Earth) in Barossa Valley and McLaren Vale. 

High in the towering eucalypt forests of the Adelaide Hills reside princesses—beautifully fresh Pinot Noir, cool-climate Syrah, Gamay, and aromatic whites of Sauvignon and Chenin Blanc. Princes of bone dry Riesling greatness camp out in Clare Valley. Dukes and Duchesses hold court in Coonawarra with herb-flecked, long-lived Cabernet. There are also all the quirky relatives, with their own distinct personalities, such as head-turning Chardonnay from the volcanic soils of Mount Gambier, and the mighty Riverland, a workhorse region with a level of renewed love via organically farmed, drought-tolerant Italian and Portuguese varieties like Nero d’Avola, Fiano, and Touriga. 

Off with the person’s head who tries to lump all South Aussie wine together into one homogenous, convenient package of “big, bold, red.” This kingdom is far more diverse than it is given credit.

Barossa

Henschke 2022 Tappa Pass Shiraz (Barossa)

A dusky, brooding Shiraz from one of Barossa’s founding wine families, this takes its time opening, but when it does, reveals a perfume of dark plum and blackberry aromas backed by sweet toasted oak, hoisin and licorice notes. The palate is mouth-filling yet silky, with lifted, quite tangy acidity, supple plum and sappy, fine tannins. You could drink this now with a decanter and protein at hand, but it should age quite beautifully for several more decades. Cellar Selection. 97 Points — Christina Pickard

$109 Armadale Cellars

Yalumba 2022 Samuel’s Collection Viognier (Eden Valley)

The pale yellow hue of this now nearly four-year-old Viognier (Yalumba’s signature white variety) hints of this wine’s richness. Aromatically, it’s more timid and tightly wound than the bombastic 2023 vintage, with honeysuckle and gentle spiced orchard fruit nuances. But it also feels more complex, perhaps more age-worthy, with a creamy, waxy texture lifted nicely by a lick of acidity. Drink only slightly chilled. 93 Points — C.P.

$20 Wine.com

McLaren Vale

Yangarra Estate Vineyard 2023 Ovitelli Grenache (McLaren Vale)

Yangarra’s celebrated winemaker, the late Pete Fraser, believed the best Grenache was made in the coolest years. This reviewer heartily agrees. 2023 was one of those years, and this ceramic egg-aged wine is shining. A pale cherry hue, juicy freshness abounds with succulent red fruit, peppery spice and a mineral core, gliding along on a silky mid-weight palate and structured with fine, cat’s tongue tannins. True to its nickname as the “Pinot of the South”, this has all the brightness and elegance you could ask for in South Aussie Grenache, with the complexity to age gracefully for another decade. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — C.P.

$80 ONE ROQ

Brash Higgins 2021 ZBO Ricca Terra Farms Zibibbo (Riverland)

This cloudy lemon-hued Zibibbo (AKA Muscat of Alexandria) is bombastically aromatic. It’s a firecracker of raw, evocative smells like the winery at crush time, with lemon pith, dandelions, mint, honeycomb and a confetti of wild herbs. The palate is slippery with a shriek of citrusy, mineral acidity akin to sucking on a lemon then licking a river stone. It walks on the wild side, but is entirely funk-free, with heaps of charisma. 92 Points — C.P.

$12 Liquor Warehouse Pro

Clare Valley

Jim Barry 2024 The Florita Riesling (Clare Valley)

An elegant, poised and still-young Riesling from a historic vineyard, this opens with lavender and pear aromas, with flickers of honey and white spice adding opulence. The palate is, for now, less about flavor and more about shape. There’s gentle richness and power, sculpted by a long, lingering, laser beam of acidity. It’s approachable enough to be food friendly now with sashimi and other delicate Japanese cuisine. But it’ll more than reward cellaring. Hold for a decade or more—it’ll be worse the wait, promise. Cellar Selection. 94 Points — C.P.

$50 Wine Empire

Koerner 2023 La Korse Red Blend (Clare Valley)

This is vibrant on all fronts, from its cherry-juice hue to its fresh red-berry, warm rock, rose-petal, turn-of-the-pepper-grinder aromas and pithy, punchy palate that’s not afraid of tannin (skins-derived rather than from oak). It is a modern, crunchy-but-serious wine made from Sangiovese with appearances from Southern Italian and French friends. It slakes across the tongue in an effortless, unmessed-with way and is, frankly, delicious. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — C.P.

$32 Wine.com

Adelaide Hills

Ochota Barrels 2022 The Price of Silence Gamay (Adelaide Hills)

Since the untimely death of Taras Ochota, who was one of Australia’s modern winemaker rock stars, his wife, Amber, has taken the reins, and wine quality hasn’t suffered. This small-batch Gamay is oh-so-delicious with enticing plump berry fruit and spice aromas and a spicy, herbal, stony mineral underlay. Piercing acidity and chiseled tannins create linearity and drive. There’s an ease and transparency to this that comes from minimal winemaking, and a drinkability that belies solid winemaking and varietal and site expression. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — C.P.

$55 Yiannis Wine

Murdoch Hill 2023 Sauvignon Blanc (Adelaide Hills)

Young, zippy and herbaceous, this aromatic Sauvignon from the Adelaide Hills offers a nose-full of white grapefruit, citrus pith and dried oregano. It’s slippery, crisp and refreshing with lovely expression of place and variety. Could developed complex honeyed notes with a bit more bottle age. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — C.P.

$21 Caviste

Victoria

If a Guinness Book of Impressive Australian Wine Records existed, the state of Victoria would sweep the floor. Despite being Australia’s second smallest state, with just 15% of Australia’s total vine acreage (compared to South Australia’s 52%), Victoria has more wine regions (21) and individual wineries than anywhere else in the country. What does this mean for the wine itself? That it’s really darn good. The plethora of independent, boutique wineries across the state make Victoria a treasure trove. Add to this the fact that its wine regions are more climactically diverse than anywhere else in Aus, and you get wine geek paradise. 

There’s the ultra maritime influence on the Mornington Peninsula. Between rounds of golf, slick Melbourne-ites can swing by any number of excellent wineries and bring bottles of precise, perfumed Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, or Pinot Gris back to their weekend homes. 

Northeast of Melbourne, the Yarra Valley has more laidback inland weekender vibes. Wine-wise, it refuses to be typecast. Fancy bright, lemony Chardonnay? You got it. Classy, long-lived Pinot Noir? Got that, too. Savory, game-y Shiraz? Spicy Cabernet? A complex Nebbiolo? 

But wait, there’s more. Mineral-rich, cool-climate wines can be found across the state, from the dramatic granite-pocked hills of the Macedon Ranges and gold rush region of Beechworth at the foot of the Australian Alps, to the sandstone mountain peaks of the Grampians, lush hills of Gippsland; the incomprehensibly old, iron-rich basalt soils of Heathcote, and the clay-loams of Prosecco-centric King Valley.

Finally, in the dry, hot inland Rutherglen region, rare fortified wines carry with them a vinous history as old as colonized Australia’s.

Yarra Valley

Giant Steps 2024 Applejack Vineyard Pinot Noir (Yarra Valley)

Raspberry cordial and damson plum are flecked in a peppery spice. There’s a hoisin note behind that; a whisper of something more stony and mineral. As it opens, layers of earthy, peppery herbs and spice unfurl from the glass. It’s silky and harmonious on the palate, the core of red fruit ensconced in fine, powdery tannins, with lovely freshness. It’s a classy drop now, but could age gracefully for another decade at least. 95 Points — C.P.

$296 Farr Vintners

Mornington Peninsula

Paringa Estate 2024 Peninsula Pinot Noir (Mornington Peninsula)

All shades of blue and purple, if we’re tasting in colors, here. This juicy Mornington Peninsula Pinot offers supple berry and savory spice—a half turn of the pepper grinder—with a stoney mineral quality beneath. The palate comes in curves and flows; it packs an acidic punch, but the core of fruit pads it out. In rush the sappy, fine tannins. Punches above its price point. 94 Points — C.P.

$76 Australia Tasting Room

Geelong

Austin’s Wines 2024 6Ft6 Pinot Gris (Geelong)

There’s an orchard blossom-honeydew melon-toasted ginger combo on the nose of this Victorian Pinot Gris that makes smelling it an enjoyable experience indeed. The palate comes with just enough acidity to keep the mouth-watering, and all those delicate aromas of the nose morphed into a gentle line of flavor that glides along on a silky-cum-pithy palate. 92 Points — C.P.

Inquire with the winery

Grampians

Mount Langi Ghiran 2024 Cliff Edge Riesling (Grampians)

From the cool, mountainous Grampians region of western Victoria, this is bright and limey with spiced green apple at the fore; an underbelly of beeswax, lamp oil and dried lavender below. In the mouth it’s zippy and fresh, with a laser beam of acidity that slices down the middle of the tongue, cutting through a slippery texture and citrusy flavor. Young and oh-so-Aussie, this will drink well (just slightly chilled) with seafood, or can age a decade or more. It’s a bargain for the quality. 93 Points — C.P.

$20 Binny’s Beverage Depot

Strathbogie Ranges

Fowles Wine 2023 Farm to Table (Victoria)

A fairly ripe, aromatic style of Sauvignon with lemongrass, lychee and just a hint of grassiness. It’s crisp and fresh, with a vibrant ping of fruit flavor. Easy pool-side sipping. 90 Points — C.P.

$19 Astor Wines

Western Australia

Size-wise, think of Australia like the U.S. if it were only populated around the edges. Western Australia would be like California—with nothing else until the East Coast. This gives you an idea of W.A.’s isolation from the rest of the country. Considering this, the fact that it’s home to one of the planet’s great wine regions is, frankly, miraculous. Three hours south of the capital city of Perth, Margaret River crafts world class Cabernets and Chardonnays that strike a balance between power and elegance, complexity and approachability, all the while reflecting their unique terroirs amidst groves of towering native eucalyptus hugging the Indian Ocean. 

There are other great wine regions in W.A. too. Delicious Grenache, Verdelho, and fortifieds in the historic Swan Valley, for example. The Rieslings and cool-climate reds of Great Southern are stellar and far-too-rarely seen Stateside. On the world stage, Margaret River is Queen of the West.

Margaret River

Vasse Felix 2024 Heytesbury Chardonnay (Margaret River)

Melon and lemon rind headline, backed by flint, toasted nuts, salted cream and subtle saline notes on this complex-yet-approachable top Chard from Margaret River’s founding estate. There’s a lip-smacking lemony freshness to the fruit, even with the subtle weight of the toasty oak and lees influence, and it lingers long and bright on the palate. A tightrope of power and elegance, this is a seriously well made Chardy from one of the region’s top players. Cellar Selection. 97 Points — C.P.

$92 Liquor Express

Leeuwin Estate 2023 Art Series Shiraz (Margaret River)

In true Leeuwin style, this southern Margaret River Shiraz offers a fragrant, pleasing pop of spiced red berry fruit reminiscent of a raspberry cordial, with fistfuls of fragrant herbs and spices. Things get a little more serious on the palate, thanks to a tug of quite powerful, spicy tannins. But this is, overall, a fresh, approachable Shiraz in a distinctive house style. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — C.P.

$33 Lay & Wheeler

Great Southern

Byron & Harold 2022 Rose & Thorns Shiraz (Great Southern)

A heady nose of chocolate covered cherries, green peppercorn, licorice and a baking spice note are lucid and lifted. There’s tart cherry and plum fruit a-plenty on the palate with a lovely lift of acidity and a spicy edge to the fine sandpaper textured tannins. A top barbecue wine that shows cool climate Shiraz in an approachable, modern light. 92 Points — C.P.

Inquire with the winery

New South Wales

You’d think that 200 years of winemaking history and a unique, food-friendly style of white wine that’s low alcohol would be more on the radars of American wine drinkers—particularly given the current white wine craze. But alas, the Hunter Valley, located two hours north of Sydney, and its distinctive Semillon remain a well-kept secret. The wine is vibrant with citrus-flower-beeswax linearity when young. Its transformation into something honeyed, even smokey, later in life is nothing short of stupendous. 

Hunter Valley makes other wines—red-fruited Shiraz, for one—and New South Wales and nearby Australian Capital Territory boast other quality-driven small wine regions (Canberra, most notably), but if you remember one thing about Australia’s east coast wine scene, it should be Hunter Valley Semillon.

Tyrrell’s 2018 Vat 1 Winemaker’s Selection Semillon (Hunter Valley)

A classic wine from a historic producer, this vintage offers a lovely, delicate perfume of honeyed citrus and waxy florals. There’s an equally waxy texture to the midweight palate; a honeyed richness creeps in to mellow out the acidity (which is not as racy as other vintages). It’s a lovely combo, an expression of place, time and variety that’s showing nice bottle age. This won’t be the longest lived “Vat 1” but it sure is drinking deliciously now. 94 Points — C.P.

$68 K&L Wine Merchants

Tasmania

This rugged island state is food-, wine-, and nature-loving heaven. Roughly the size of Ohio, “Tas” is located 150 miles off the southeast coast of Australia. It has, in recent years, become one of the most sought-after corners for winemaking in Australia. Prized for being the climactically coolest region in Aus, large scale national and international wine businesses have snapped up land, expanding the state’s vineyard footprint five-fold in the last two decades. Plenty of small grower-winemaker operations exist here, too. Like elsewhere, they’re driving the quality revolution on the “Apple Isle.” With precise, traditional method sparkling, slinky Pinot Noir, ethereal Syrah, fleshy-yet-delicate Riesling, and exuberant, saline Chardonnay—across subregions that span the eastern half of the island—Tasmania represents cool-climate winemaking at its finest.

Small Wonder Wines 2024 Chardonnay (Tasmania)

Tasmania’s cool climes shine through on this youthful Chardonnay. Melon and cashew nuts are underpinned by something creamier and more saline, like nougat and seashells. It’s round and rich on the palate yet fresh, too, with a toasty, vanillin note to the finish. A modern Aussie Chard that makes for versatile food pairing. 91 Points — C.P.

$27 Convive

House of Arras 2017 Rosé Sparkling Blend (Tasmania)

A very pale pink color, this vintage Tassie rosé offers toasty, rich buttered shortcrust pastry aromas amidst honey, peach and wild strawberry nuances. It’s bone dry with power but plenty of freshness thanks to a rush of crackling acidity from first sip. Vigorous bubbles are just behind. It leaves the tongue tingling and ready for more, finishing on a dried herbal, medicinal note. 93 Points — C.P.

$476 Farr Vintners

title: If the Knicks Win, Josh Hart Knows Exactly What to Drink
summary:
The Knicks are in the NBA Finals for the first time in over 25 years. Here's what the small forward suggests drinking if they win.

The post If the Knicks Win, Josh Hart Knows Exactly What to Drink appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
New York hasn’t tasted an NBA Championship since 1973, but after more than five decades teetering between heartbreak and hope (and heartbreak, again), the Big Apple might finally be on the verge of basketball greatness. 

The Knicks are in the NBA Finals for the first time since 1999, and Game One tips off tonight in San Antonio against a young Spurs team seeking to prove their dominance. As NYC holds its breath waiting for the on-court battle to start, there’s an energy around the city that feels foreign—one that has grown in strength with every Knicks win this postseason. This combination of optimism and hunger is anchored by the confidence that this team can, and will, do great things.

Nobody is more locked in on this playoff run than small forward Josh Hart. The fan favorite has been one of the shining stars of this Knicks’ resurgence, delivering incredibly clutch performances in must-win games in front of MSG’s demanding crowds.

Hart is also a wine guy. It’s not a stretch to say that the NBA at large has become obsessed with wine, and Hart is no exception, telling us last December about his most prized bottle: a 1995 Domaine de la Romanée-Conti La Tâche Pinot Noir from Burgundy. Hart even has an Instagram account dedicated to photos of his sprawling wine collection.

So, if the Knicks do emerge victorious in the Finals, how does an oenophile like Hart celebrate? On a recent episode of his podcast, “Roommates Show,” co-hosted with fellow Knicks star Jalen Brunson, Hart tackled the topic when a fan called in to ask for specific wines to toast a potential Knicks championship with. See below for Hart’s picks, as well as where to find them, divided into tiers by average price.

Josh Hart recommends:

$90 and Under:

Heitz 2021 Cabernet Sauvignon (Napa Valley)

$90 Astor Wines

$250 and Under:

Château Montrose 2022 Saint-Estèphe

This is a powerful wine with bold tannins and a rich structure. The wine’s ripe blueberry aroma and dense texture go well with the concentrated black fruits and opulence. The wine will take its time: drink from 2029. Cellar Selection. 97 Points — Roger Voss

$250 Wine.com

$400 and Under:

J.L. Chave 2022 Red (Hermitage)

$298 Zachys

Dal Forno Romano 2016 (Amarone della Valpolicella)

As this wine is poured you can sense the care and meticulous work that went into to craft it. Aromas of plum and blackberries merge with clove, anise and licorice roots as sweet tobacco notes waft by. Bold, firm and structured the palate is a statement. Driven now by dark fruit flavors the wine will evolve into a more savory and spice filled room as the wine ages. 97 Points — Jeff Porter

$387 Mr. D Wine Merchant

Quintarelli 2018 Red (Amarone della Valpolicella Classico)

$335 Woodland Hills

$1000 and Under:

Château Mouton Rothschild 1999 Pauillac

“I love Mouton Rothschild, so I’ll go with something with some age, like anything in the ’90s or early 2000s…. Possibly a ’99.” — Josh Hart

$525 Berry Bros. & Rudd

Château Mouton Rothschild 2016 Pauillac

The rich fruit in this wine nearly envelops the tannins. Flavors of black plums, blackberries and blueberries meld with intense acidity to mask the power and concentration of the polished tannins. With this structure, will age for many, many years. Do not drink before 2026. Cellar Selection. 98 Points — R.V.

$1,000 Wine Express

Over $1000:

Château Le Pin 2008 Pomerol

“That’s what I drank the other day [at his postgame press conference after winning the Eastern Conference Finals]. That was elite, I think it was the ’08.” — Josh Hart

$4,376 Tribeca Wine Merchants

Domaine de la Romanée-Conti 2004 La Tâche (La Tâche)

Although this wine is very firm and closed at the outset, it opens—and keeps opening in the mouth—to offer great fruit flavors. It is finely balanced, very pure, combining an ethereal character with the proper feet-in-the-soil taste of great Pinot Noir. Age for 20 years, if you can bear to wait. Cellar Selection. 96 Points — R.V.

$5,995 Acker Wines

Domaine de la Romanée-Conti 2004 Romanée-Conti

This wine’s considerable power derives from its a solid texture, its multifaceted red-berry flavors, juiciness, freshness and perfect balancing acidity. You can, and will want to, hold this in the mouth for many minutes. It is a complete wine. 97 Points — R.V.

$19,500 JJ Buckley
